As you'll no doubt hear over the next year, 2011 marks 150 years since the start of the Civil War, and there is no song that more closely evokes that fight than ""The Battle Hymn of the Republic."" Julia Ward Howe wrote those famous words, mine eyes have seen the glory of the coming of the Lord, in 1861.
And since then, the song's become a kind of second national anthem.

Dominic Tierney talks about the history of the song, the Elvis and the Judy Garland versions, for example, in a new issue of The Atlantic, which, incidentally, is where it was originally published in 1862.
Dominic, welcome to the program.
Hi, how are you doing?
Thanks for having me.
Was this meant to be a song specifically for the Union's cause during the Civil War?

Well, yes and no. Of course, as you probably know, the song began actually as a campfire spiritual back in the 1850s called ""Say, Brothers, Will You Meet Us."" And then even in these days before the Internet, it went viral and turned into a song called ""John Brown's Body.
And this was partly about the famous John Brown, the antislavery terrorist, but it was also about a regular soldier in the Union army who happened to share the same name as John Brown.
And in 1861, Julia Ward Howe, who was an abolitionist and a poet, was visiting troops near Washington, D.C.
and the party was bored in their carriage ride and so they began to sing these Union songs, among which was ""John Brown's Body.
And one of the members of the party, a reverend, liked the tune but felt that the lyrics were a little unelevated.
For example, one of the lyrics went: We'll hang ole Jeff Davis from a sour apple tree.
So, he wondered if how could he craft something a little more fitting.
And that night, Howe went to sleep and when she woke the next morning, the lines of a poem began to form themselves in her mind.
And so she jumped out of bed and scribbled them down.

And the Atlantic Monthly, as you said, ended up paying Howe $5 and publishing the poem in 1862, giving it a title ""The Battle Hymn of the Republic."" And it's quite clearly about the Civil War but it's also a timeless song that speaks much more broadly to great themes in the American soul.
I'm going to ask you about that.
But first, I'm going to play a few versions that we've actually found.
This is Judy Garland appearing on CBS.
I think it's 1963.
One of the greatest songs that was ever written is very seldom done on television, and I would like to sing that song for you tonight.
(Soundbite of song, ""The Battle Hymn of the Republic"")
(Singing) Mine eyes have seen the glory of the coming of the Lord.
He is trampling out the vintage where the grapes of wrath are stored.
Glory, glory, hallelujah...
That is a glorious glory there.
She was also trying to make a political point, right?
Right, exactly.
The ""Battle Hymn"" is actually bound up with all three of the great assassinations of the 1960s.
Martin Luther King's final words in public came from the ""Battle Hymn.
Dr. MARTIN LUTHER KING JR. (Civil Rights Activist): I'm not worried about anything; I'm not fearing any man.
Mine eyes have seen the glory of the coming of the Lord.
The next day he was dead.
And the last song at Bobby Kennedy's requiem mass was the ""Battle Hymn.
Wow.
You know, people are tired of pretending like we're not angry.
And for the first time - at least in my living memory - we're seeing some real sort of large-scale consequences for some men who have, allegedly, engaged in really predatory behavior.
So there's a lot of power in unleashing anger, especially anger that's been suppressed for so long.
Because the perception is angry men are strong, but angry women are viewed as hysterical, vindictive and downright scary.
That has quelled those voices for a long time.
You are seeing something different now.
Yeah.
I mean, traditionally, we punish women for losing their temper in public.
Women are supposed to be compliant and helpful and nice and, you know, play the support role for men who are the real actors in the world.
And women who do lose their temper in public, we call them crazy.
And we try to destroy them to a certain degree.
You know, it's really hard for me to even come up with any examples of women who had what we call a meltdown who were met with a positive response and taken seriously.
I mean, we don't - meltdown is almost a gendered word, you know?
Does it feel that that is changing now?
I don't know.
I mean, there's still a lot of hostility.
It's hard to say whether it's being taken more seriously or received differently because there is still a huge backlash.
I mean, I'm still seeing all the reasons why maybe these women misunderstood what happened to them.
Or...
Blowing it out of proportion.
Blowing it out of proportion - or it was a different time.
He was raised in a...
Right.
...Different time when this was fine, as though all of our fathers and grandfathers were sexual predators.
Right.
And they weren't.
What is the role of men in this moment?
The role of men in this moment is to listen and to think critically about their behavior and to, most importantly, change their behavior.

On a broader scale, I think it's really important for men to think about how much space they take up and to step aside and make some room for women, especially women of color - marginalized women who get, you know - who are far disproportionately affected by these forces.

He says the ancients who built Carhenge back in 1987 didn't know about this eclipse.
It was a local named Jim Reinders who wanted to build a version of Stonehenge as a tribute to his dad.
Now, the original Stonehenge in England was used to mark celestial events.
It was built of giant rocks that prehistoric people dragged for miles from the quarries.
But Jim was an engineer, a practical-minded guy.
So he decided, if we build it out of cars, the wheels on them would greatly simplify the logistics.
